audit_trail.notes: Two full review cycles completed. In cycle 1, gemini raised a specific algebraic mismatch claim (T_old ≠ T_GUP exactly at minimum mass) and assigned a major-flaw verdict. In cycle 2, codex consulted the arXiv PDF (the HTML bundle had all equations rendered as empty placeholders by LaTeXML), found the equivalence algebraically consistent under the paper's parameter identification, and gemini retracted the algebraic-mismatch objection. The HTML equation-rendering failure was a persistent audit limitation throughout both cycles and is noted as a structural auditability problem for the HTML source. All three reviewers converged in cycle 2 on the extremal-horizon interpretation of the zero-temperature cancellation. The Bekenstein bound vs. Smarr conflation objection remained partially disputed between gemini and codex and is flagged for human spotcheck. No reviewer formally verified the GEVAG field equations for the radially varying metric ansatz; this is the most consequential unresolved technical question.

general_reader_takeaway: This 6-page paper proposes that a modified theory of gravity called GEVAG can fix a known inconsistency in quantum-corrected black-hole physics: instead of black holes stopping evaporation at a hot "leftover" state (a finite-temperature remnant), the paper argues that correctly accounting for a running gravitational coupling drives the temperature smoothly to zero. The review board found the mathematical sketch internally consistent but built on an assumption the theory does not derive — that the gravitational coupling can be extended off the black-hole horizon — and the key "remarkable cancellation" at the minimum mass turns out to be a standard geometric consequence of the type of metric the assumption generates, not an independent confirmation. The result is an interesting conditional model observation, but it falls well short of the robust resolution of the remnant problem that the title and conclusions imply.
human_spotchecks: Verify whether the standard Hawking temperature formula T = f′(r_h)/4π needs to be re-derived when G is promoted to a spatially varying function G_eff(r) in the near-horizon region — specifically, whether additional terms arise from the Killing vector normalization or from modifications to the surface gravity under a position-dependent coupling.

Check whether the near-horizon metric f(r) = 1 − 2M·G_eff(r)/r, with G_eff(r) determined by the logarithmic entropy correction, can be derived from or matched against the GEVAG effective gravitational field equations, and whether it corresponds to a physically acceptable effective stress-energy source.

Confirm whether the 'Bekenstein constant' C_B defined in Eqs. (22) and (27) correctly captures the standard Bekenstein bound S ≤ 2πER/ℏc (which involves energy, entropy, and the physical horizon radius as independent quantities) or whether it reduces to a Smarr-relation coefficient that tests only the scaling properties of the black-hole equation of state.

Assess whether the off-shell continuation A(r) = 4πr² is uniquely preferred among geometrically natural choices (e.g., dynamical trapping horizon area, Kodama–Hayward construction, Painlevé–Gullstrand areal radius), and whether alternative continuations produce materially different correction terms or endpoint temperatures.

Determine whether the first law dE = T dS requires additional work terms from the variation of G_eff in the GEVAG setting — for instance via a Noether-charge analysis — and whether omitting such terms changes the expressions for thermodynamic energy and the Bekenstein constant.
public_summary:
The paper operates within the GEVAG (Generalized Entropy Varying-G) framework, in which a generalized black-hole entropy implies an area-dependent effective gravitational coupling G_eff(A). Prior companion work by the same author showed that this reproduces the heuristic GUP (Generalized Uncertainty Principle) black-hole temperature for logarithmic entropy corrections. The present paper argues that if G_eff is additionally promoted to be valid in the near-horizon neighborhood — not just on the horizon itself — then differentiating the metric function by the product rule yields a second temperature contribution δT. For the logarithmic entropy correction, the paper claims this δT exactly cancels the GUP-like first term at the minimum mass, so the full temperature goes to zero rather than leaving a finite-temperature remnant. General formulas for the thermodynamic energy and a "Bekenstein constant" are also derived for arbitrary entropy corrections, and a connection to RG-scaling naturalness is proposed.

Two review cycles with three independent reviewers (claude, codex, gemini) found the local algebra of the T_old–T_GUP equivalence to be consistent upon inspection of the PDF (a cycle-1 algebraic-mismatch objection was retracted after PDF verification). The deeper structural concerns, however, survived both cycles. The central off-shell extension of G_eff is explicitly acknowledged by the paper to have no field-equation support within GEVAG, and it is not unique: other natural choices for the off-shell area function A(r) would produce different correction terms and potentially different endpoint temperatures. The Schwarzschild-like metric with radially varying G(r) is inserted by analogy and is not checked against the effective GEVAG gravitational equations or a consistent stress-energy source. The standard surface gravity formula T = f′(r_h)/4π is applied without re-derivation in a setting where G varies spatially, and it is not established that no additional Killing-norm terms arise. Critically, all reviewers in cycle 2 concurred that the "remarkable cancellation" producing T = 0 at minimum mass is the standard extremal-horizon condition: the chosen metric f(r) = 1 − 2M G_eff(r)/r becomes degenerate (f′(r_h) = 0 algebraically) at the minimum mass, so zero surface gravity follows by construction, not as independent evidence for the off-shell assumption. The paper uses this tautological result to retroductively validate the assumption, which the reviewers flag as circular. Additionally, the thermodynamic energy and Bekenstein constant are derived from the unmodified first law, but in varying-G theories additional Noether-charge or work-term corrections may be required. Whether the "Bekenstein constant" C_B correctly implements the Bekenstein bound or is instead a Smarr-relation coefficient was disputed between reviewers and remains unresolved without the rendered equations. The paper situates the zero-temperature endpoint among many other quantum-gravity models that produce the same behavior, but does not supply evaporation dynamics, heat capacity, or backreaction analysis to establish that the endpoint is actually reached.
reviewer_disagreements: Algebraic equivalence of T_old and T_GUP (Eqs. 7–8): Gemini in cycle 1 raised a specific numerical counter-example claiming an exact mismatch at the minimum mass. Codex checked the PDF in cycle 2 and found the equivalence is algebraically consistent under the paper's parameter identification (c₁ = π G α). Gemini retracted the objection in cycle 2. This dispute was resolved in favor of the paper's algebra.

Bekenstein constant vs. Smarr relation: Gemini (cycles 1 and 2) argues that C_B = E/(TS) is the Smarr-relation coefficient and not a proper implementation of the Bekenstein bound S ≤ 2πER. Codex (cycle 2) partially disputes this, noting that the paper's definition C_B = 2πf(A)/(Af′(A)) is not simply E/(TS), so the specific Smarr-conflation charge is not confirmed. Both reviewers agree that the Bekenstein-bound interpretation requires clarification, but the precise nature of the problem is unresolved.

Technical soundness scoring: Gemini and claude (cycle 2) assign technical soundness ≈ 3/10; codex (cycle 2) assigns 5/10. The difference reflects codex's greater weight on the internally consistent algebra (after PDF inspection) versus the other reviewers' heavier weighting of the unvalidated metric ansatz and off-shell extension.

Overall significance: Codex regards the conditional observation as a neat, incremental model-level result worth publishing in a specialized venue; gemini and claude are more skeptical, viewing the result as largely tautological given the extremal-horizon interpretation.

strongest_objections.objection: The off-shell extension of G_eff from the horizon into the near-horizon neighborhood is explicitly acknowledged by the paper to have no field-equation basis within GEVAG. The theory defines G_eff only on the horizon and provides no dynamics for it elsewhere. The choice A(r) = 4πr² (Schwarzschild areal coordinate) is natural but not unique; other geometrically reasonable continuations would produce different derivative terms and potentially different endpoint temperatures. The entire main result depends on this ungrounded assumption.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section II, discussion before and around Eq. (10)

strongest_objections.objection: The Schwarzschild-like metric f(r) = 1 − 2M·G_eff(r)/r with radially varying G_eff(r) is inserted by analogy with the standard Schwarzschild solution and is not shown to solve the GEVAG effective gravitational field equations or to correspond to a consistent effective stress-energy source. Without this check, the surface-gravity calculation is geometrically formal but physically ungrounded.

The standard Hawking temperature formula T = f′(r_h)/4π is derived for metrics with a constant gravitational coupling G. Promoting G to a spatially varying function G(r) may modify the Killing vector norm and hence the surface gravity, potentially adding further terms that the paper does not compute. Applying the unmodified formula to the new metric may incompletely account for all contributions.

The paper frames the exact cancellation T_GUP + δT = 0 at the minimum mass as a 'remarkable cancellation' that retroductively validates the off-shell extension. However, with the chosen G_eff(r), the minimum-mass configuration corresponds to a degenerate (extremal) horizon of the effective metric, for which f′(r_h) = 0 by the mathematical definition of a degenerate root. The zero temperature is therefore a guaranteed consequence of engineering an extremal-horizon limit in the metric ansatz, not an independent prediction.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section II Eq. (20); Section IV paragraph beginning 'Given that an extra assumption…'

strongest_objections.objection: The thermodynamic energy and Bekenstein constant are derived using the unmodified first law dE = T dS, but in theories with a varying gravitational coupling additional Noether-charge or work terms typically appear in black-hole thermodynamics. The paper does not verify that these extra terms vanish in the GEVAG context, leaving the derived expressions potentially incomplete.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section II Eq. (21); Section III Eqs. (25)–(26)

strongest_objections.objection: The paper claims T → 0 at the minimum mass resolves the GUP remnant problem and describes an 'effective remnant that slowly evaporates asymptotically.' However, showing zero surface gravity at one mass value does not establish the actual evaporation trajectory: luminosity, greybody factors, heat capacity, and backreaction analysis are required to determine whether the black hole reaches this endpoint or has a divergent evaporation time.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section II after Eq. (20); Fig. 1 caption; Conclusion

strongest_supported_claims: Within the chosen near-horizon ansatz (Schwarzschild-like metric with G_eff(r) = r²/(r²+α)), the algebra linking the unmodified GEVAG temperature T_old to the standard GUP temperature T_GUP via the author's parameter identification is algebraically consistent, as verified by consulting the PDF (cycle 2, codex).

Kinematically, promoting G_eff to a function of r and differentiating the metric function by the product rule does generate a second temperature term δT involving dG_eff/dr, given the static metric ansatz.
General formulas for Hawking temperature and thermodynamic energy for arbitrary entropy correction f(A) follow from the first law in outline, provided f'(A) > 0 and denominators remain finite.

The paper correctly situates the zero-temperature endpoint qualitatively among a broad class of quantum-gravity models (noncommutative geometry, asymptotic safety, loop quantum gravity, string theory) that produce regular black holes with T→0 final states.
The connection between GEVAG's running G_eff and the running Newton coupling of asymptotic safe gravity is noted in the companion literature and is a coherent observation within the GEVAG framework.
technical_takeaway: The paper claims that extending the GEVAG horizon-defined coupling G_eff(A) to a near-horizon radial function G_eff(4πr²) adds a derivative correction δT to the Hawking temperature, so that the full temperature T = T_GUP + δT vanishes at the minimum mass for logarithmic entropy corrections. Cross-review established that: (1) the algebraic equivalence of T_old and T_GUP (Eqs. 7–8) is consistent under the author's parameter identification, after consulting the PDF; (2) the zero-temperature cancellation at minimum mass is the expected extremal-horizon condition — a degenerate horizon satisfies f′(r_h) = 0 by definition, so the result is not an independent validation of the off-shell assumption; (3) the metric ansatz with spatially varying G(r) is not shown to solve the GEVAG effective field equations; (4) the standard Hawking temperature formula T = f′(r_h)/4π may require re-derivation when G is spatially varying; and (5) the first law in a varying-G setting may carry additional Noether-charge terms not accounted for in the thermodynamic energy and Bekenstein constant derivations. The RG-scaling interpretation of the Bekenstein bound is explicitly loose and unconnected to an actual RG flow.
